title: The Macro Minute: Does the ceasefire signal a RORO phase transition to a risk-on Market Regime?

Happy Wednesday out there, team 42. It's
your skipper here, Darius Dale, to
present our macro minute for Wednesday,
April 8th, 2026. Hope everyone's having
a great week. So, as always, we'll start
with the executive summary from today's
lead-off morning note. So, let's dive
right in. Today's key macro question is,
does the US-Israel-Iran ceasefire signal
a pending risk-on
market regime? For those maybe new to
our nomenclature, risk-on phase
transition just means you're
transitioning from risk-on regime to
risk-off regime or vice versa.
Uh these are, you know, the catalysts
that cause the maximum amount of
position changing uh from a factor and
asset allocation perspective across the
global buy side, and it's very important
as an investor if you're looking to stay
on the right side of market risk um to
take advantage of these flows, to
recognize these flows as quickly as
possible, uh particularly when they
change cuz they usually the trends
usually last, uh and take advantage of
those flows to maximize upside capture
in bull markets or to take advantage of
those flows, to front-run those flows uh
in terms of getting out so that you can
minimize uh downside capture uh in in in
bear markets. Uh so, uh in terms of the
answer to our question, uh likely is the
answer to the question, does this
ceasefire signal a pending risk-on phase
phase transition to a risk-on market
regime? Uh the mere fact that Iran was
willing to concede safe passage through
the Strait of Hormuz for 2 weeks to
negotiate the terms of a broader
settlement indicates that Iranian
leadership was only using the world's
most critical shipping corridor as
negotiating leverage. Here are the most
important things we learned today
regarding the six key macro cycles that
influence the momentum and dispersion
within and across asset markets on a
trend basis. Um nothing actionable from
the perspective of the growth,
inflation, monetary policy, and fiscal
policy cycles. So, we'll start with
positioning and then we'll end on
liquidity.
So, in terms of positioning, last night
the US and Iran secured a 2-week
ceasefire and committed committed to
reopening the Strait of Hormuz,
signaling a pivot towards negotiations
over further military escalation. While
de-escalation triggered sharp declines
in oil and gas prices alongside a global
risk-on rally, unresolved divergences
across UN and Iran US-Iran negotiating
positions will likely contribute to
persistent uncertainty throughout the
negotiations.
President Trump indicated forthcoming
sanctions relief and reaffirmed that
there would be no enrichment of uranium.
His pivot marked a stark reversal from
his prior warning on Tuesday night that
a whole civilization will die tonight,
never to be brought back again, and that
was his quote, not mine,
absent Iranian capitulation.
Both Washington and Tehran are framing
this ceasefire as a victory, yet
material gaps in their respective
in-game requirements persist and
threaten longer-term resolution.
President Trump disclosed the receipt of
a 10-point Iranian proposal, describing
as a quote, workable basis on which to
negotiate, while withholding full
disclosure of prospective agreement
terms. Iran's uh Supreme National
Security Council outlined demands,
including sovereign control over the
Strait of Hormuz, recognition of its
nuclear enrichment program, removal of
all primary and secondary sanctions, and
full withdrawal of US combat forces,
each a hardline stance that may be
paired back as negotiations progress.
Alternatively, such hardline stances may
not need to be paired back to the extent
the US administration is seeking to form
a joint venture with the Iranian
leadership to extort a toll on cargo
passing through the Strait of Hormuz.
This outcome would represent a historic
geopolitical victory for the Trump
administration uh and a historic L for
those of us who doubted quote, Taco
Trump's ability to succeed in the ever
complicated Middle East with this
chaotic part of the deal framework. That
would That would That would garner way
more than a golf clap if they were able
to just to muscle in and and start
start charging quote-unquote protection
through the Strait of Hormuz. That would
be quite the geopolitical victory. So,
you have to give the administration uh
plenty of credit uh for that and I I
certainly will uh to the extent that is
the outcome. Uh All told, agreeing to
reopen the Strait of Hormuz, even if
just on a transitory basis of 2 weeks,
uh by agreeing to re-open Strait of
Hormuz, uh Tehran just signaled that its
ultimate goal is not to destroy the
global business cycle nor risk a global
famine by continuing to embargo 20 to
50% of the world's petroleum products
and fertilizer inputs. We can speculate
as to what Tehran's ultimate goals are
and they will likely resemble the
five-point plan they previously
proffered.
But we can surmise that tanking the
global economy and causing a global
famine is not on the list.
This removes a significant degree of
left tail risk from global financial
markets. And whenever left tail risk is
removed from the distribution of
probable economic policy market
outcomes, the net result is a mean and a
median that is more positive than
previously anticipated. And so if you
get a more positively skewed return
distribution relative to the starting
point, you're going to cause positioning
to change across the global buy side.
People are going to increase their gross
exposure, widen their net exposure, and
that's going to cause other funds to do
the same because everyone has quant Not
everyone. All the best funds have
quantitative risk management
overlays.
Getting into the liquidity cycle,
Vice President J.D. Vance stated in
Budapest that President Trump's
fulfilled US fulfilled US military
objectives by seeking to quote decimate
Iran's conventional war capacity while
cautioning that the ceasefire remains
fragile. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth
also asserted that the comprehensive
success of Operation Epic Fury citing
destruction of Iranian ballistic missile
capabilities, elimination of naval
threats to regional shipping lanes, and
degradation of Iran's defense industrial
base despite evolving US war
justifications. In our view, it's clear
the administration is using the
ceasefire as a well-timed off-ramp from
the conflict ahead of the midterm
elections. Moreover, the two-week
ceasefire framework allows for an
extension via mutual consent through
Iranian state media although Iranian
state media emphasized negotiations will
proceed under conditions of quote
complete distrust. Speaking of fragility
and distrust, President Trump announced
via Truth Social plans to impose 50%
tariffs on US imports from any nation
supplying weapons to Iran, likely to
create additional leverage with China
ahead of his meeting with President Xi
Jinping on May 14th and 15th in Beijing.
Additionally, a drone strike targeted
Saudi Arabia's East-West oil pipeline,
critical for bypassing the Strait of
Hormuz. Meanwhile, regional hostilities
persisted across Kuwait, the UAE, and
Iran despite the ceasefire with Hormuz
traffic still
elevated shipping risk assessments.
We're clearly not out of the woods yet.
All told, the US-Israel-Iran ceasefire
will likely lead to a binding agreement
that unwinds the global liquidity
crisis. Investors should expect
negotiations to be tense and full of
brinkmanship,
but the ultimate outcome is likely one
that features a lumpy trend higher in
petroleum products and fertilizer inputs
being shipped through the Strait of
Hormuz.
This implies a global liquidity crisis
that was catalyzed by the restricted
supply of these commodities and a
resulting hit to global dollar recycling
flows will dissipate over the next few
weeks and months as well.
As all As always, so NA equals nothing
actual with respect to our base and
priors for the growth inflation and
monetary policy fiscal policy cycles,
continue reviewing to learn whether our
institutional grade risk management
overlays KISS and Dr. Mo are evolving
their signals in response to these or
anticipated fundamental dynamics.
